heading: Assessment
html:
Measure blood pressure using validated equipment and correct technique. Confirm diagnosis with ABPM (preferred) or HBPM unless immediate severe hypertension requires urgent action.

  • Use both-arm clinic measurement at diagnosis and use the arm with the higher persistent reading.
  • If clinic BP is 140/90 mmHg to 180/120 mmHg, confirm with ABPM/HBPM.
  • Classify stage and identify severe hypertension (clinic systolic 180 mmHg or higher, or diastolic 120 mmHg or higher).
  • Baseline work-up: urine ACR and haematuria dipstick, HbA1c, U&Es, creatinine/eGFR, lipids, fundoscopy, and 12-lead ECG.
  • Assess cardiovascular risk and consider secondary causes when suggested by age, phenotype, or resistant pattern.

heading: Primary care management
html:
The default approach is structured lifestyle support plus stepwise antihypertensive treatment with active safety monitoring.

  • Lifestyle: weight optimisation, healthy diet, regular exercise, sodium reduction, alcohol reduction, smoking cessation, and reduced excess caffeine.
  • Start treatment: treat persistent stage 2 hypertension; treat higher-risk stage 1 hypertension (target organ damage, CVD, renal disease, diabetes, or elevated 10-year CVD risk).
  • Step 1: ACE inhibitor or ARB for most under 55s or people with type 2 diabetes; CCB first line for age 55 and over (without type 2 diabetes) or relevant Black African/African-Caribbean family origin; use thiazide-like diuretic if CCB not tolerated.
  • Step 2: combine 2 classes (ACEi/ARB plus CCB or thiazide-like diuretic) after adherence and dose review.
  • Step 3: ACEi/ARB plus CCB plus thiazide-like diuretic at optimal tolerated doses.
  • Step 4 resistant hypertension: confirm persistent elevation with ABPM/HBPM, reassess adherence and secondary causes, then consider low-dose spironolactone if suitable potassium/renal profile.
  • Monitoring targets: clinic BP below 140/90 mmHg if under 80, and below 150/90 mmHg if 80 or over; home/ambulatory targets are 5 mmHg lower.
  • Safety: monitor renal function and electrolytes after starting or escalating ACEi/ARB/diuretic therapy and review adverse effects at each step.

heading: Who to refer
html:
Urgent same-day specialist review

  • Clinic BP 180/120 mmHg or higher with retinal haemorrhage or papilloedema (accelerated hypertension).
  • Clinic BP 180/120 mmHg or higher with life-threatening symptoms (for example chest pain, new confusion, heart failure features, or acute kidney injury features).
  • Suspected pheochromocytoma or unstable secondary-cause presentation.

Routine specialist referral

  • Resistant hypertension despite optimised stepwise therapy and confirmed adherence.
  • Suspected secondary hypertension requiring specialist investigation.
  • Adults under 40 with confirmed hypertension for secondary-cause and long-term risk-benefit assessment.

Include in referral

  • Confirmed clinic and ABPM/HBPM readings and trend.
  • Current and previous antihypertensive medicines with doses, tolerance, and adherence summary.
  • Recent renal function, electrolytes, urine ACR, ECG, fundoscopy findings, and comorbidity/risk profile.

heading: Who not to refer
html:
Do not routinely refer adults with uncomplicated stage 1 or stage 2 hypertension that is controllable in primary care, with no red-flag symptoms, no suspected secondary cause, and acceptable treatment tolerance.

Continue primary care management with structured follow-up, treatment titration, and annual review once blood pressure is stable.
